linked_sections.text_snippet: A complex spectrogram of the noisy speech waveform y ∈ R D is computed via an STFT. The input to the feature encoder Y in ∈ R T × F × 2 is the compressed magnitude spectrum ( Y m ) c ∈ R T × F extracted via powerlaw compression concatenated with the wrapped phase spectrum Y p ∈ R T × F .

This experiment uses a subset of 20 signals from the dataset (2 speakers, 1 male and 1 female, 10 signals each). The original sounds represent clean speech excerpts with the length ranging from 1.6 to 3.8 seconds, with no significant portions of silence, sampled at 48 kHz.

papers.title: Multi-Modal Face Anti-Spoofing Based on Central Difference Networks
papers.abstract: Face anti-spoofing (FAS) plays a vital role in securing face recognition systems from presentation attacks. Existing multi-modal FAS methods rely on stacked vanilla convolutions, which is weak in describing detailed intrinsic information from modalities and easily being ineffective when the domain shifts (e.g., cross attack and cross ethnicity). In this paper, we extend the central difference convolutional networks (CDCN) \cite{yu2020searching} to a multi-modal version, intending to capture intrinsic spoofing patterns among three modalities (RGB, depth and infrared). Meanwhile, we also give an elaborate study about single-modal based CDCN. Our approach won the first place in "Track Multi-Modal" as well as the second place in "Track Single-Modal (RGB)" of ChaLearn Face Anti-spoofing Attack Detection Challenge@CVPR2020 \cite{liu2020cross}. Our final submission obtains 1.02$\pm$0.59\% and 4.84$\pm$1.79\% ACER in "Track Multi-Modal" and "Track Single-Modal (RGB)", respectively. The codes are available at{https://github.com/ZitongYu/CDCN}.

The paper, however, addresses multi-modal face anti-spoofing using Central Difference Networks (CDCN) to detect presentation attacks on face recognition systems. The paper, however, addresses 'Face Anti-Spoofing (FAS)' to secure 'face recognition systems' from 'presentation attacks' using 'multi-modal' data (RGB, depth, infrared). In contrast, the paper addresses multi-modal face anti-spoofing (FAS) to secure face recognition systems from presentation attacks, utilizing central difference networks on RGB, depth, and infrared modalities. These are distinct problem domains (audio deepfake detection vs. face anti-spoofing) and employ different types of input data and core methodologies.

papers.title: Regularized autoregressive modeling and its application to audio signal reconstruction
papers.abstract: Autoregressive (AR) modeling is invaluable in signal processing, in particular in speech and audio fields. Attempts in the literature can be found that regularize or constrain either the time-domain signal values or the AR coefficients, which is done for various reasons, including the incorporation of prior information or numerical stabilization. Although these attempts are appealing, an encompassing and generic modeling framework is still missing. We propose such a framework and the related optimization problem and algorithm. We discuss the computational demands of the algorithm and explore the effects of various improvements on its convergence speed. In the experimental part, we demonstrate the usefulness of our approach on the audio declipping and dequantization problems. We compare its performance against state-of-the-art methods and demonstrate the competitiveness of the proposed method in declipping musical signals, and its superiority in declipping speech. The evaluation includes a heuristic algorithm of generalized linear prediction (GLP), a strong competitor which has only been presented as a patent and is new in the scientific community.

These are fundamentally different research problems with distinct goals and methodologies. | Method: The user's research idea proposes a system for real-time deepfake audio detection using a binary classifier trained on spectrogram features. In contrast, the paper focuses on regularized autoregressive modeling for audio signal reconstruction, specifically addressing problems like declipping and dequantization. There is no methodological overlap between training a classifier for deepfake detection and using AR models for signal reconstruction. | Domain: The user's idea is in the domain of deepfake audio detection, which falls under audio signal processing and machine learning. The paper focuses on autoregressive modeling for audio signal reconstruction, specifically audio declipping and dequantization, which are also within audio signal processing. While both deal with audio signals, the specific application targets (deepfake detection vs. signal restoration) are different sub-areas. | Contribution: The user's research idea focuses on detecting deepfake audio using a binary classifier on spectrogram features. In contrast, the paper's contributions are centered on developing a general framework for regularized autoregressive (AR) modeling and applying it to audio signal reconstruction problems like declipping and dequantization. These are distinct problems with different methodologies and goals.

papers.title: MambAttention: Mamba with Multi-Head Attention for Generalizable Single-Channel Speech Enhancement
papers.abstract: With new sequence models like Mamba and xLSTM, several studies have shown that these models match or outperform the state-of-the-art in single-channel speech enhancement and audio representation learning. However, prior research has demonstrated that sequence models like LSTM and Mamba tend to overfit to the training set. To address this, previous works have shown that adding self-attention to LSTMs substantially improves generalization performance for single-channel speech enhancement. Nevertheless, neither the concept of hybrid Mamba and time-frequency attention models nor their generalization performance have been explored for speech enhancement. In this paper, we propose a novel hybrid architecture, MambAttention, which combines Mamba and shared time- and frequency-multi-head attention modules for generalizable single-channel speech enhancement. To train our model, we introduce VB-DemandEx, a dataset inspired by VoiceBank+Demand but with more challenging noise types and lower signal-to-noise ratios. Trained on VB-DemandEx, MambAttention significantly outperforms existing state-of-the-art discriminative LSTM-, xLSTM-, Mamba-, and Conformer-based systems of similar complexity across all reported metrics on two out-of-domain datasets: DNS 2020 without reverberation and EARS-WHAM_v2. MambAttention also matches or outperforms generative diffusion models in generalization performance while being competitive with language model baselines. Ablation studies highlight the importance of weight sharing between time- and frequency-multi-head attention modules for generalization performance. Finally, we explore integrating the shared time- and frequency-multi-head attention modules with LSTM and xLSTM, which yields a notable performance improvement on the out-of-domain datasets. Yet, MambAttention remains superior for cross-corpus generalization across all reported evaluation metrics.

papers.title: Unified Cross-modal Translation of Score Images, Symbolic Music, and Performance Audio
papers.abstract: Music exists in various modalities, such as score images, symbolic scores, MIDI, and audio. Translations between each modality are established as core tasks of music information retrieval, such as automatic music transcription (audio-to-MIDI) and optical music recognition (score image to symbolic score). However, most past work on multimodal translation trains specialized models on individual translation tasks. In this paper, we propose a unified approach, where we train a general-purpose model on many translation tasks simultaneously. Two key factors make this unified approach viable: a new large-scale dataset and the tokenization of each modality. Firstly, we propose a new dataset that consists of more than 1,300 hours of paired audio-score image data collected from YouTube videos, which is an order of magnitude larger than any existing music modal translation datasets. Secondly, our unified tokenization framework discretizes score images, audio, MIDI, and MusicXML into a sequence of tokens, enabling a single encoder-decoder Transformer to tackle multiple cross-modal translation as one coherent sequence-to-sequence task. Experimental results confirm that our unified multitask model improves upon single-task baselines in several key areas, notably reducing the symbol error rate for optical music recognition from 24.58% to a state-of-the-art 13.67%, while similarly substantial improvements are observed across the other translation tasks. Notably, our approach achieves the first successful score-image-conditioned audio generation, marking a significant breakthrough in cross-modal music generation.

papers.title: Multi-Input Multi-Output Target-Speaker Voice Activity Detection For Unified, Flexible, and Robust Audio-Visual Speaker Diarization
papers.abstract: Audio-visual learning has demonstrated promising results in many classical speech tasks (e.g., speech separation, automatic speech recognition, wake-word spotting). We believe that introducing visual modality will also benefit speaker diarization. To date, Target-Speaker Voice Activity Detection (TS-VAD) plays an important role in highly accurate speaker diarization. However, previous TS-VAD models take audio features and utilize the speaker's acoustic footprint to distinguish his or her personal speech activities, which is easily affected by overlapped speech in multi-speaker scenarios. Although visual information naturally tolerates overlapped speech, it suffers from spatial occlusion, low resolution, etc. The potential modality-missing problem blocks TS-VAD towards an audio-visual approach. This paper proposes a novel Multi-Input Multi-Output Target-Speaker Voice Activity Detection (MIMO-TSVAD) framework for speaker diarization. The proposed method can take audio-visual input and leverage the speaker's acoustic footprint or lip track to flexibly conduct audio-based, video-based, and audio-visual speaker diarization in a unified sequence-to-sequence framework. Experimental results show that the MIMO-TSVAD framework demonstrates state-of-the-art performance on the VoxConverse, DIHARD-III, and MISP 2022 datasets under corresponding evaluation metrics, obtaining the Diarization Error Rates (DERs) of 4.18%, 10.10%, and 8.15%, respectively. In addition, it can perform robustly in heavy lip-missing scenarios.

existing_examples.name: Deepfake Audio Detection Research
existing_examples.description: Numerous academic and industry research papers and projects focus on detecting deepfake audio using machine learning, including binary classifiers on spectrograms or other audio features. This is a very active research area.

existing_examples.name: Voice Biometrics/Anti-Spoofing Systems
existing_examples.description: Existing voice biometric systems often incorporate anti-spoofing measures to detect synthesized or replayed speech, which is a related problem to deepfake detection. While not always real-time deepfake specific, the underlying techniques are similar.

existing_examples.name: Commercial Deepfake Detection Services
existing_examples.description: Several companies (e.g., Pindrop, Sensity AI, Reality Defender) offer solutions for detecting synthetic media, including audio deepfakes, often integrated into enterprise security or fraud prevention. Some of these aim for real-time or near real-time analysis.

matched_sections.text_snippet: Following the previous research in unifying image-language-speech modal translation [12], we formulate music modal translation as a seq2seq task using vector-quantized discrete tokens of image and audio.
matched_sections.reason: Both approaches involve processing audio into discrete units for a machine learning model. The user's idea uses 'spectrogram features extracted from short audio segments' for a binary classifier, while the paper uses 'vector-quantized discrete tokens of image and audio' for a seq2seq task. The underlying concept of discretizing audio for model input is similar.

matched_sections.paper_title: Unified Cross-modal Translation of Score Images, Symbolic Music, and Performance Audio
matched_sections.heading: Data Filtering
matched_sections.text_snippet: For model training, we only use segments that are shorter than 20 seconds in audio and smaller than 256,000 total pixels in image.
matched_sections.reason: The paper explicitly mentions using 'segments that are shorter than 20 seconds in audio' for model training, which aligns with the user's idea of extracting features from 'short audio segments'.

layer1_results.paper_title: Multi-Input Multi-Output Target-Speaker Voice Activity Detection For Unified, Flexible, and Robust Audio-Visual Speaker Diarization
layer1_results.reason: Problem: The user's research idea focuses on detecting deepfake audio in real-time voice calls, which is a problem of authenticity verification. In contrast, the evaluated paper addresses speaker diarization, which is the task of identifying 'Who-Spoke-When' in conversational data, as stated in the introduction. These are fundamentally different research problems with distinct goals. | Method: The user's research idea focuses on detecting deepfake audio using a binary classifier on spectrogram features. The paper, however, proposes a Multi-Input Multi-Output Target-Speaker Voice Activity Detection (MIMO-TSVAD) framework for audio-visual speaker diarization, which aims to identify 'Who-Spoke-When' using both audio and visual modalities. There is no mention of deepfake detection, binary classification for deepfakes, or the specific use of spectrogram features for deepfake detection in the paper. | Domain: The user's idea is in the domain of deepfake audio detection, specifically for real-time voice calls. The paper focuses on speaker diarization, which aims to identify 'Who-Spoke-When' in conversational data. While both involve processing audio (and the paper also video) in real-time scenarios and use machine learning, the core application targets are distinct: deepfake detection versus speaker identification and segmentation. | Contribution: The user's research idea focuses on detecting deepfake audio in real-time voice calls using a binary classifier on spectrogram features. In contrast, the paper proposes a Multi-Input Multi-Output Target-Speaker Voice Activity Detection (MIMO-TSVAD) framework for speaker diarization, aiming to identify 'Who-Spoke-When' in multi-speaker scenarios, which is a different problem domain and contribution.

sentence_level.sentence: A system that detects deepfake audio in real-time voice calls by training a binary classifier on spectrogram features extracted from short audio segments, distinguishing synthesized from genuine speech.

matched_sections.paper_title: Multi-Input Multi-Output Target-Speaker Voice Activity Detection For Unified, Flexible, and Robust Audio-Visual Speaker Diarization
matched_sections.heading: INTRODUCTION
matched_sections.text_snippet: L IKE documenting events in a diary, speaker diarization is the task of automatically detecting multiple speakers' utterance boundaries in conversational data [1]. It aims to split the audio or multi-modal signals into segments with labeled identities, solving the problem of 'Who-Spoke-When.'
matched_sections.reason: The user's idea is about 'detects deepfake audio in real-time voice calls' and 'distinguishing synthesized from genuine speech', which falls under the broader domain of speech processing and analysis, similar to the paper's focus on 'speaker diarization' in 'conversational data'. Both deal with analyzing speech in real-time or conversational contexts.

linked_sections.paper_title: MambAttention: Mamba with Multi-Head Attention for Generalizable Single-Channel Speech Enhancement
linked_sections.heading: Model Overview
linked_sections.text_snippet: A complex spectrogram of the noisy speech waveform y ∈ R D is computed via an STFT. The input to the feature encoder Y in ∈ R T × F × 2 is the compressed magnitude spectrum ( Y m ) c ∈ R T × F extracted via powerlaw compression concatenated with the wrapped phase spectrum Y p ∈ R T × F .
linked_sections.reason: Both the user's idea and the paper mention the use of 'spectrogram features' extracted from audio. The paper details the computation of a complex spectrogram and the extraction of magnitude and phase spectra as input features.

linked_sections.paper_title: Regularized autoregressive modeling and its application to audio signal reconstruction
linked_sections.heading: Speech declipping
linked_sections.text_snippet: To strengthen the generality of the proposed framework, an extension of the previous comparison focuses on declipping of speech. This experiment uses a subset of 20 signals from the dataset (2 speakers, 1 male and 1 female, 10 signals each). The original sounds represent clean speech excerpts with the length ranging from 1.6 to 3.8 seconds, with no significant portions of silence, sampled at 48 kHz.
linked_sections.reason: The paper explicitly mentions working with 'speech' signals, which is a key component of the user's idea (deepfake audio in real-time voice calls).

The closest related work focuses on general domain applications, suggesting your specific application to music generation is a key differentiator.
